speaker: interviewer
text: S-C-H-W-A-R-T-Z. This is roll number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Schwartz, that song was really beautiful. And I would like-- I would like you to tell us in English the, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The last word is [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH]. That means outside it's cold and windy. And that is the mother's voice talking to herself. And she is saying she is-- she feels like Moses' mother felt, that she had no choice. And she left her child on strange hands-- strange arms to live with.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the bottom line. That's the way she felt. She lost her son. But she gave her son a chance to survive. It was the last, the last meaning. I mean, I understand the words because I speak Yiddish. [INAUDIBLE]. OK. How-- who was the, the-- you remember that song very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn it? There at the time, [? was a-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard that song that young man-- he was a young man-- sing it as a concert. And somehow, it stuck in my memory. I don't know why. I mean, he sang a few songs. And I cannot-- don't remember the words.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when you say that song, the other song-- there was a song also, which my granddaughter was in a play. And she had to do just what the other song said. She says, Grandma, I am in a play. And I'm doing something which is not important enough. I says, what do you mean? She told me in the song, her mother is standing there. Pack. Pack the clothes. You're in charge of taking the clothes. While we were taking away, we knew we were [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd love-- I would like very much to hear that, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just want to-- I just want to tell you the few words about the song.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: That song, what it said is pack in another towel, another sweater, in case there is cold. There is another song which goes with it. All right, what was your question?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were helping those people, those young people that came into your town and, and, and they came out-- were coming out of the sewers, how did they know that that was a place--

speaker: interviewee
text: From what I understood after the war, there were [? chayalim. ?] And there was in Poland all over underground. They were trying to take out the youth as many as they could. There were a lot of people involved helping them out to get the young people. There was, we foun-- I found out after the war quite a few non-Jewish families were hiding those people, people or kids. So mostly the [? chayalim, ?] but also working with the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the underground, which they call-- we call them partisans, all right? The partisans took them out. And from there, we were-- it's partly the [? chayalim. ?] They were working with us. And the [? chayalim ?] took them to Israel or whatever. And they-- they were on their way to Israel. How come-- you were a young girl. How come you didn't go with them on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't up to me. I don't know. I've been asking the same question. How come-- I didn't belong to anyone. How come they didn't include me?

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, it never occurred to me why did they take them? Why didn't they take us? Because my mother's friend-- she was [? Volkoce--. ?] She had two children. She would have been very happy to send her two children with me to Israel or wherever to take them away. Somehow, it never came up.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, where I came from, they were-- they kept things away from the children. They did not want not to expose them that it's something bad. Everything is perfect. Everything will work out. So I asked my question, the same question, a million times. But I have no answers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interesting. And where did you go after?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was something, 1944-- must have been like April, I think March, April, that they finally took us to the ghetto, the [? Kosice ?] ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the day-- how, what happened that day? What time of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in spring. It was in the springtime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I know we celebrated Passover in the ghetto. In ghetto, we were working. I was working in a brick factory with carrying bricks, fixing up bricks. And we were-- they put us all up in a factory. And of course, we're already on the the floor. They did give us-- we had blankets at that time with us. So we were there. I don't remember-- we were not staying there for very-- for very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? In the brick factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the brick factory. From there they--

speaker: interviewer
text: First you stayed in the ghetto. Then you went to brick factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The brick fact-- we were in the ghetto and working in a brick factory. Now, the ghetto was in the brick factory. So we were working--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the sleeping conditions? Did you stay with a family that you were staying with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were stay-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All, all.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: All together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them had cots. But most of us were sleeping on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how the ghetto looked and the accommodations, or which streets it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I wouldn't know. I was a stranger over there. So I really don't know how big the ghetto was. But I remember there was a very big, like long barracks. And they were-- we put on sheets to separate families from families. But your family had a little two sheets on both sides. And there you were, like staying on-- laying on the floor. Any other facilities, I don't remember. And what the food was, I have no idea. I just don't-- can't recall what it's-- what's what. Somehow, the food wasn't important. It was just part of living. And we were just hoping tomorrow will be better, something new.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked, within walking distance from where the camp was. It was in walking distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trucks-- I, I think it must have been the Germans which they took us from the-- from the poor-- from the children's home to the ghetto on trucks. They loaded us up on trucks, in open trucks. And I don't remember how far it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any belongings? Could you take anything at that time with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, we-- the main thing that we took was backpacks. Used to call them [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. They just came up here in, in America. We packed then. And I think that one suitcase, they allowed you to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as much how we had the bedding in there, we just took the essentials-- I mean, one change of clothes. We had to to bedding, something warm, something to keep us warm. And we also sneaked in some food, which was all make toast in case, you know, something did-- toast and fruit. So from your home at the moment to the ghetto you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: From--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the home-- I wasn't in the home. I was living in, in the [? poor. ?] I was living in the children's home. I was living there. She used to travel home, back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was living there. It was really nice. They-- they gave me a little room. And it was very nice, because she lived in a small apartment. And just-- there wasn't enough room for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I stayed in there. So she was with me all day-- all day long. And in the morning, she came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it gave me the evenings that I'd let us study. I-- when you are young, you don't think of it. You just keep yourself busy. Maybe that's why I have the claustrophobia now, staying in that little tiny, room. Never-- never thought about it this way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So from the-- in the ghetto, you use to work in the brick factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And not for too long, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was just a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, they loaded us on trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trains. And we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. They put us in trains. And we were traveling. It was quite, quite a days or week, or a week or so, we were in the train. They were cattle trains. And the facilities in the cattle trains which we had was a blanket holding by two ladies. At that time, we were separated already-- ladies and men separate-- with a bucket, whatever you had to go for your-- Food I don't remember. The food, whatever we had, we took it along with us. But I don't remember them feeding us. So every time at, at night when the train stopped, somebody had to empty bucket. The facilities, the trains were so small that they made us-- we had, we had an SS man staying by the door with a rifle in case somebody decide to jump off. And at night, we had to lay down. It so tight that we had-- that we were like herring, her-- put together. If one turned, everybody turned. But-- and we had some straw. They were very nice to us. They gave us some straw on the train, so at least we were not flat. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the same car all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the s-- I don't remember. But I remember when we got off in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know you were going to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know where we were going. We were just in the train. We were young kids. We had there a group kids. We, we were working in the factory. We were learning new songs and carrying on. We made, we made a party. And always Jews, when we get together, two, three of them, we make a party. We sing. And we dance. And we carry on. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the brick factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the brick factory. We had there some actors that were teaching the songs. So while we were working, we had to carry bricks from one room to the other. We made a chain. And we're just throwing the bricks to one another. While we're throwing, we're singing. So we made--

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Made a joke out of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of those songs? I know you like to sing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It went like this. [SINGING IN HUNGARIAN] That's Hungarian. I live in the brick factory. And my hair is covered with dust. But we were kids at that time. With that-- we made the best of it. Let's put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had there two actors. And they were entertaining us while they were also working, they made-- make it look like fun. In fact, when we went on the train, they made up a song right away. They went like this. [SINGING IN HUNGARIAN]. I'm gonna explain it to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train is moving. The chimney-- the smoke is coming out of the chimney. And it's taking away all the Jews. And who knows if we will ever-- whoever is coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, the grinding on the wheels? So we were singing, sitting thee and singing. Because all day long, we didn't do nothing in the train. We had to amuse ourselves with something. So this is

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know, like you made the best of the situation that we have. And I always did. I guess that's why I am where I am. If not, I would have never survived. When we get out of the train in Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days after was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been like at least more than a week. I really don't remember, because we traveled at night. And sometimes the trains in the daytime, they were just staying in the stations. They did not want, when there was a air raid, that there is a train going to Auschwitz, so depended when. Sometimes they were traveling early in the morning and late at night and all through the night. In the daytime, sometimes they did travel during the day. But sometimes they were just staying in the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of the year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The spring. It was just after Passover. Must have something around May or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got to Auschwitz. How did you find out it was Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Auschwitz, we got off the train. And there were the SS men. And [INAUDIBLE] I wish I would made it my business to remember the names. And they were just staying the way they are dressed, and with a stick, you, here. You, here. You, here. They were just picking you one, two, three. And of course, if you had somebody, you wanted to be s-- with somebody. If they wanted to go like with a sister, with a mother, or with dad, and they-- right away, you could feel the-- oh, they hit them with a club. Or they hit them with a gun. Or they hit them there that they were able to remain on the floor, which they didn't go to any line at all. They were [? buried ?] off. Well, when I got to Auschwitz, I was staying with my mother's friend, Mrs. [? Vokosh, ?] her daughter, and myself. I wasn't very long in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before changing from Auschwitz, could you please tell me a little bit? You just said that you remember how they were dressed, the SS men? Can you describe it to me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were wearing the tall boots. Sp-- pants were like riding, like riding pants. And clothes were, from what I remember, I think green and gray, and the hats, those SS hats. And of course, the big SS were right on their, on their sleeves. And they have the [INAUDIBLE] medallions hanging on, on their jackets. That's all I can describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's really good. After you got off the train and you were put in different lines, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to a bathhouse. Going from the train to the bathhouse, that was the worst experience which anybody can, can describe. We were all walking in a line. And there were people behind the bars already. And we were thinking, who are those people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Their heads were shaven. And they were wearing striped clothes. And that's all what kept saying [HUNGARIAN SPEECH]? Where are you from, in Hungarian. And we couldn't believe that this is our people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought they was some kind of a criminals. Because like I said, Mrs. [? Vokosh ?] kept us in the dark. She never wanted to talk about it-- what's what, what's ahead of us-- one day at a time, wait. Well, she was also a very religious person. One day at a time-- God will help you. Don't worry. Everything will be OK. Well, they took us to the bathhouse. They shaved our heads, our privates. They put us in the showers with some kind of a disinfectant. And just that shaving wasn't too pleasant, because the men did the job. And here I was staying and looking at my friend. I didn't recognize her without the hair. Then they gave us dresses, which were gray and blue dresses, after the shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dresses with what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stripes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in stripes, gray and blue stripes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gray and blue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gray and blue-- of course, they gave us whatever came first. Somebody got dresses they were above the knees. Somebody got dresses over the ankle. But we kept-- after that, we switched them around, what's what. They didn't care what was happening. By the way, they took out all our belongings remained on the train. They never allowed us to take them. They did not allow us to take our belongings. And some people who tried to sneak away something with us, once they took away everything from us, they had to leave them where the-- when they cut our hair. And they took our clothes-- clothes, jewelry, whatever you had left. Then we went in to one of the barracks that was in Auschwitz. I don't remember which camp. We were there for two nights. Then they moved us-- moved us to another one closer to the crematorium, they told us. And then we went to a third one, which we were sure that was our last night there. They put us in a room, which the floor was wet. They had washed it before, in cement. And you better sit down or lay down. If not, you are being shot. That's all what we had. There was a little window. The little window was there. They had a light constantly going on us, round and round, to check how everybody is. And they were sitting and having a good time, the Germans. And they were telling us, you better have a good time tonight. That's the last night you're here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by a good time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were drinking and eating, the SS men, in that little room-- at the guard house. Whatev-- well, they made fun of us, all right? They made fun of us, you know, called us all kind of a names. This is the last night. Tomorrow, you will see. You will have a nice [INAUDIBLE]. So, now they had there--

speaker: interviewer
text: They would speak to you in, in which language?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you understood German?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German-- we understood German. We speak Yiddish. We understood German. But we thought it was our last night. And there was a few religious women. And they said prayers with us. All night long, we're praying. They all said the [? Viduy-- ?] you know, if you know, before the dying. I don't know-- and the religious people, you say [? Viduy ?] before-- If you know you are going, you are ready to accept.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were not allowed to sing, but quietly, whispering, were praying all night long. Nobody slept. As it happened, the next morning they took us all out. And they lined us up outside. And they called. And they told us, line up according to your alphabet, what your name is with.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we just didn't think straight. It was just so numb. So my friend tells me, look, your, your name starts with H, which is [? Halpert. ?] Ours starts with F. So you have to go to the, to the line which is with the H. Well, that was the time which I was more alone than ever in the world. But we were-- I was lucky because my line, the H, they took us on trains. And they took us-- we went down to Riga. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Riga is in-- let me see-- Lithuania. We came into Lithuania. There was a big camp. And there were people from the vicinities who they took them in. It was also a ghetto. And they still had their hair and their regular clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they still had it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had their regular clothes. When we came there, they all said, who are you? Are you prisoners? I mean, they were in the ghetto, but none of them-- not many people really knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw your friends again? Or you got at this point separated from your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never saw them again. No, I never saw them again. But when were in-- when you are with people and you have nobody, you make friends. You learn. You make friends. While we were there, we worked different, different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Riga. They took us to the boat. That-- that's by the big port. They took us to unload boats, unload trains. And then another time, they-- we worked in a factory which they called [? Anoden. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: They called it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Anoden. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Anoden? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Anoden. ?] We took [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? All ?] [? right. ?] We took the old batteries. And we cut them to took out the powder. You take an old battery which we use for anything. You cut out the back. And then you split it. And there is a little black powder. So they needed the powder for the munition. So there were three shifts working that every eight hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we worked. And, and our shift was-- our shift was, was at night. They took us there by, by truck, open truck. We came back-- whoever came back was black from the dust. Well, we used to make fun of the people when we went in the morning to work and they were coming. Where did you work? They were black, which forget about showers. If you were able to get washed up, you were very lucky. So the people who worked at night were sleeping in the daytime. They gave us-- they gave us lots of soup when we came in. They gave you soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us soup. The portion usually was soup and like two slices of bread. That was your da-- your daily allowance. So if you ate up the soup and the bread for the day, you didn't eat nothing till the next 24 hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if you were satisfied with the soup, save the bread-- if they didn't steal it from you overnight. Because when you put it under your pillow, you didn't have anything that [? stopped ?] people. I was lucky, because we had bunks. I had bunks. So at nights, while I was working, somebody else sleeped-- slept in it, which-- Oh. So it was a bunk for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a bunk for myself. They were like the three tiered bunks, one on top of the other. And, and you had a bunk. So when, when one the girls-- when I was working, she slept in it. And then when she was working, I slept in it. I mean, we didn't have our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you always came to the same one?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the same bunk, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was-- where was the location of--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where in the camp was the sleeping--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were barracks. They were barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're military barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many girls they put in, in in one barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking-- in a-- we just one in a bed. You only had one in a bed. But being there were three shifts, so there was something or somebody came in early, somebody [? left. ?] So we did shared. We had to share, two of us, the same bed. And we were lucky because we had-- I don't remember what kind of mattress-- but we had a blanket and a pillow. So we were very lucky. And of course, if another girl came in, we had to share the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothing did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still lived in the gray and blue stripes. And if you're gonna ask me what kind of shoes, I don't remember what we had. I think they might have let us keep the shoes. And then they-- there were holes. And then we had to put newspaper in it to keep it from going. They never gave us new shoes. But it wasn't bad there. We were happy. We had a, a place where we had to stay. And then when the war started to get a little bit closer, the people who were there in the ghetto, they start taking them away. Then they start separating, which they blamed us for coming there. They started-- they separated them to send them to Auschwitz, the older people. When we got in that camp, it was a camp, so-- but there were people who were there before. They still had their own clothes. So one morning, they called up all those people for an appell. And they separated them. And they send them away. Very few of them remained. Only the young people remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to change the tape now and go to the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Try to do my best. It's not easy.
